food_name: Red Kidney Beans, Cooked from Dried (1 cup, 177g)
region_of_origin: Mexico and Central America; widely adopted globally including South Asia and Southeast Asia
food_subgroup: Legumes — cooked dried beans; red kidney beans cooked from dried, 1 cup = 177g

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for cooked kidney beans as a food product; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 1 cup (177g) of cooked red kidney beans, providing approximately 225 kcal, 15.4g protein, 11.3g dietary fibre, and 230mcg folate.

nutrient_source.title: Beans, kidney, red, mature seeds, cooked, boiled, without salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173744)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values correspond to USDA SR Legacy entry for red kidney beans, cooked, boiled, without salt, per 100g. All per_100g values are taken directly from this source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — kidney beans (red, cooked from dried)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 24 (low) for red kidney beans cooked from dried is directly referenced in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (24 × 40.4g) / 100 ≈ 9.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high potassium-to-sodium ratio; near-zero sodium content
hypertension.guidance: Red kidney beans provide approximately 713mg potassium and only 2mg sodium per cup (177g), yielding one of the most favourable potassium-to-sodium ratios of any food, making them a DASH diet pillar food that actively supports blood pressure reduction.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI 24) and low glycemic load (GL 9.7 per cup); high resistant starch and soluble fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of 24 and GL of 9.7 per cup, red kidney beans produce a minimal postprandial glucose response; their resistant starch also exerts a documented 'second meal effect', reducing blood glucose after subsequent meals, making them highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High soluble fibre, very low saturated fat, high folate and potassium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Red kidney beans contain 11.3g dietary fibre per cup (of which a significant fraction is soluble fibre that lowers LDL cholesterol), 0.18g saturated fat, 230mcg folate (supporting homocysteine metabolism), and 713mg potassium, collectively supporting cardiovascular risk reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High folate content supports methotrexate therapy; anti-inflammatory mineral profile
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Red kidney beans provide 230mcg folate per cup (58% RDA), which is clinically relevant for patients on methotrexate therapy for rheumatoid arthritis, while their high magnesium and potassium content contributes anti-inflammatory mineral support.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Red kidney beans cooked from dried (1 cup, 177g) are a nutritionally dense legume with one of the lowest glycemic indices of any food (GI ~24, GL ~9.7 per cup). They are prepared by soaking dried beans overnight and then boiling vigorously at 100°C for at least 10 minutes — a full rolling boil is mandatory to destroy phytohaemagglutinin (PHA lectin), a heat-labile toxin present in raw and undercooked kidney beans that causes acute vomiting and diarrhoea within 1–3 hours of ingestion. Slow cookers must NOT be used to cook dried kidney beans from raw without pre-boiling, as they may not reach the required temperature. Canned kidney beans are fully pre-cooked and safe to eat directly. Nutritionally, one cup provides approximately 225 kcal, 15.4g protein, 0.9g fat, 40.3g carbohydrate, 11.3g dietary fibre, 3.9mg iron, 230mcg folate, 713mg potassium, and only 2mg sodium. The red seed coat contains tannins that provide additional alpha-amylase and alpha-glucosidase inhibitory activity, further blunting postprandial glucose. Kidney beans are a DASH diet pillar food due to their exceptional potassium-to-sodium ratio. They are widely used in Indian rajma curry, Latin American chili con carne, grain bowls, and canned baked beans. In Singapore, they appear most commonly in North Indian rajma curry at Indian restaurants and in canned baked beans (Heinz). They are vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: are kidney beans good for blood pressure

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy (exact match for red kidney beans, cooked, boiled, without salt). GI value of 24 is directly referenced in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2) are defensible direct matches. All mandatory multilingual names are present.
notes: Cholesterol is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy — kidney beans are a plant food and contain no dietary cholesterol. Vitamin A (as RAE) is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for cooked kidney beans. Vitamin B12 is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy — plant foods do not contain vitamin B12. Vitamin D is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. Trans fat is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.04g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.27g) are sourced from USDA SR Legacy detailed fatty acid data. water_g estimated at 67.0g per USDA SR Legacy (100g − dry matter). CRITICAL FOOD SAFETY NOTE: Raw or undercooked kidney beans contain phytohaemagglutinin (PHA lectin) which causes acute gastrointestinal illness; dried kidney beans must be boiled at a full rolling boil (100°C) for at least 10 minutes before consumption; slow cookers must not be used without pre-boiling. Canned kidney beans are pre-cooked and safe. The food_group has been set to 'Meat and alternatives' consistent with legume classification in nutritional frameworks (legumes serve as plant-based protein alternatives). GL per serving calculated as (24 × 40.4g available carbohydrate per 177g serving) / 100 = 9.7.
